---
title: "sea level end cretaceous"
author: "Sidney"
date: "2024-11-26"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see [http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com](#0){.uri style="font-size: 11pt;"}.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

## Review of literature that look at the sea level curve around the K/Pb. 

-   Miller et al., 2005\
    Phanerozoic record of global sl. Good review but resolution is way too low.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
-   Signor & Lipps
"During periods of regression, when no deposits of marine sediments are
developed on continental areas, there is no possibility of finding the last
occurrences of taxa which go extinct during the regression. 
This will have the effect of pushing artificial range truncations further back
in time to when there is a record to sample.
If the regressions are gradual, they could have the effect of amplifying the
apparent gradual nature of the extinctions, 
simply by reducing sample size as the regression progresses, 
at least in continental sequences." 
Matsumoto, 1980, found that the K/P extinction occurred during a major
regression. Perhaps this is the first person to find this? Therefore, 
worth mentioning. Nearly everywhere in the world, there is an 
unconformity during the K/Pb.
"Hallam (1971) documented a decline in the areal extent of 
Late Cretaceous rocks, relative to the abundant mid-Cretaceous rock record.
From this alone we would expect to find a gradual increase in the apparent
extinctions, regardless of the actual pattern of mass extinctions." 
The gradual decrease of species diversity before the K/Pb includes marine and
terrestrial biotas.

"Evidence contradicting a catastrophic mass extinction might not be derivable from fossil range data alone. Because the disappearance of taxa from the fossil record may not be due to simultaneous extinction, the range data mustw be supplemented with other information, for example the existence of numerous deposits where the taxa should be found but are not."


-   Miller et al., 2020\
    Cenozoic sea level. \
    Sl from ~66 Mya to recent. Based on Mg/Ca paleotemperatures and δ18O. Milankovic scale resolution, 1 data point every
    10 ky. Reproduce-able data. But perhaps does not predate the K/Pb.

-   Jaiprakash et al., 2016\
    Forams in canyon in India.\
    Looks at the absence/presence of foram species in wells across a canyon. They show a hiatus starting at \~69.5 Mya. This lasts between 1.8 Mya and 9.1 Mya depending on the well. Meaning, it's often longer than the runtime of CarboKitten and will be difficult to compare to model results. Only the timing of the 1.8 Mya hiatus could make a usefull comparison for in the discussion. They do not plot the sl. They do mention "sl fall began at the boundary of planktic zones CF6-CF5 (69.6 Ma) (Nagendra et al., 2002, 2011)." This exposed the Kallankurichhi Formation at 68.8 Ma, and remain exposed to this day. The sl fall was of the order of 100m. In some wells, the hiatus starts \~69 Ma, in some wells later \~67.5 Ma.

-   Ray et al., 2019\
    Short-term Cretaceous sl change.\
    "No consensus currently exists regarding the magnitude of Cretaceous short-term (less than 3 Ma in duration) eustatic sea-level change."\
    Potentially reproduce-able SL change for the Cretaceous. Could perhaps use Miller et al., 2020 and Ray et al., 2019 combination. This paper shows timing of transgression and regression (and SL) during Cretaceous according to 3 other papers. The locations that document estimates of short-term SL are Canada (Northern Alberta), New Jersey, Spain, France, Denmark, Yemen and Egypt. Ray et al., 2019 also shows a great overview of who studied what part of the Cretaceous and how, i.e., Backstripping, Fossil assemblages etc.\
    They say that the maximum short-term sl change during the Maastrichtian is less than 60m.
    The overall data coverage of SL change of the Santonian to the Maastrichtian is relatively poor. 
The SL change magnitude highly depends on the research method.
Backstripping leads to magnitudes between 1 & 40 m. 
Foraminiferal studies (both in New Jersey margin) leads to magnitudes of 25 to 75 m in the Campanian. 
The transition into Maastrichtian has higher magnitudes. 
Backstripping estimates from the New Jersey margin indicate 4 to 57 m.
Foraminiferal studies indicate 50 to 70 m. 
Facies variations and foraminiferal studies in Western Egypt 
(El-Azabi & Farouk, 2011; Jain & Farouk, 2017) indicate magnitudes between 15 and 60 m within the Maastrichtian.
The Maastrichtian contains SL changes of higher magnitudes. 
Santonian to Campanian contains  magnitudes at the upper limit of approx. 40 m,
whereas the Maastrichtian represents a shift to maximum magnitudes limits of approx. 50 m.

- El-Azabi & Farouk
  Study in the Kharga Oasis in Wester Egypt. They find a well-defined hiatus at
  the K/Pg boundary marked by a conglomerate. the latest Maastrichtian through
  to the mid-Danian biostratigraphic zones are absent.They find that the shale
  on each side of the boundary is very different. the lower shale unit is
  "restricted, shallower marine sub-tidal facies, devoid of planktonic
  foraminifera but containing a few agglutinated benthonic foraminifera of low
  species diversity". "The upper unit is an open marine, deeper sub-tidal
  facies flooded with well-diversified planktonic and benthonic foraminifera.
  The number of planktonic foraminifera is 80-90% higher than that of
  benthonic foraminifera."
  
- Jain & Farouk 2017 state that the boundary is at a time of transgression.
  According to their SL the hiatus should be before the boundary (still late
  Maastrichtian) during the Falling Stage system Tract and forced regression.
  They call the time before the boundary where there is SL rise, Highstand
  system tract. And the maximum SL, which falls slightly after K/Pb is a
  Transgressive System Tract to them. But if you'd ask me the SL shows that
  the time directly before the K/Pb clearly shows a Transgressive System Tract
  followed by a Highstand System Tract after the K/Pb, so the other way round.
  But who am I.

  The SL in fig.10 seems to fall a couple MY sooner than in fig.11. 
  The data seems reproduceable, however, I don't fully understand the           supplementary material.

- Haq 2014
  Mentions in the appendix who studied the Maastrichtian eustatic evenets and
  where. Events Events KMa3 (69.4 Ma), KMa4 (68.8 Ma), and KMa5 (66.8 Ma) are
  the 3 most recent Maastrichtian eustatic events. KMa3 can be tract in
  western European basins (1), on the Arabian Platform (2), in New Jersey (3)
  and in O2-isotopic trends. KMa4 can be tract in Western Europe (1), the
  Arabian Platform (2) and in New Zealand (4). KMa3 & KMa4 have an amplitude
  of 25-75m. KMa5 can be tract in Western Europe (5), on the Arabian Platform
  (2), in Tunisia (6), on the New Jersey margin (3), and in New Zealand (4).
  KMa5 has an amplitude of ~75 m, Haq calls this "medium amplitude".

1. Hardenbol, J., Robaszynski, F., 1998. Introduction to the Upper Cretaceous. In: de Graciansky, P.-C., et al. (Eds.), Mesozoic and Cenozoic Sequence Stratigraphy of European Basins. SEPM Special Publication, 60, pp. 329–332.
NO SEA LEVEL CURVE
Hardenbol, J., Thierry, J., Farley, M.B., et al., 1998. Mesozoic and Cenozoic sequence chronostratigraphic framework of European Basins. In: de Graciansky, P.-C., et al. (Eds.), Mesozoic and Cenozoic Sequence Stratigraphy of European Basins. SEPM Special Publication, 60, pp. 3–13 (Chart 5, “Cretaceous Chronostratigraphy”).
NO ACCESS

2. Haq, B.U., Al-Qahtani, A.M., 2005. Phanerozoic cycles of sea-level change on the Arabian
Platform. GeoArabia 10, 127–160.
NO SEA LEVEL CURVE

3. Miller, K.G., Sugarman, P.J., Browning, J.V., et al., 2003. Late Cretaceous chronology of large, rapid sea-level changes: glacioeustasy during the greenhouse world. Geology 31, 585–588.
NO ACCESS
Miller, K.G., Kominz, M.A., Browning, J.V., et al., 2005b. The Phanerozoic record of global sea-level change. Science 312, 1293–1298.
REPRODUCEABLE!!!! Resolution of 5 kyrs. 

4. Crampton, J.S., Schioler, P., Roncaglia, L., 2006. Detection of Late Cretaceous eustatic signature using quantitative biostratigraphy. Geol. Soc. Am. Bull. 118, 975–990.
NO ACCESS

5. Haq, B.U., Hardenbol, J., Vail, P.R., 1988. Mesozoic and Cenozoic chronostratigraphy and cycles of sea-level change. Soc. Econ. Paleontol. Mineral. 42, 71–108.

6. Li, L., Keller, G., Adatte, G., Stinnesbeck, W., 2000. Late Cretaceous sea level changes in Tunisia: a multi-disciplinary approach. Geol. Soc. Lond. 157, 447–458.
